schools.summary: Gill: the divine threatening after Bathsheba's sin now takes effect, the judgment continuing from the child's death into Amnon's crime.
Keil-Delitzsch and Ellicott trace Nathan's prophesied judgment and fix the family relations that make the sin incest.
Henry: grace does not run in the blood but corruption does; the devil sows the unnatural lust in Amnon's heart.
Clarke establishes that Tamar was Absalom's full sister but Amnon's half-sister only.
Gill: Amnon is straitened and perplexed by the unbridled lusts raging within him.
Clarke (via Delaney) and Wesley: unlawful passion is uniquely wasting, and Tamar's guarded virginity denies Amnon any opportunity.
Ellicott renders it 'it seemed impossible'; the king's children lived apart, and Tamar's seclusion left Amnon no opening.
Gill: the 'friend' Jonadab proved in the end to be Amnon's ruin.
Henry: Jonadab had more of David's blood than of David's spirit, a subtle man cunning for any bad design.
Clarke calls the counsel diabolic and denies that the 'former days' were any better.
Ellicott: 'subtil' means sagacity used rightly or wrongly; he fixes the genealogy from Shammah/Shimeah.
Keil-Delitzsch: 'from morning to morning' notes the hour when the sick look worst, aiding the pretence.
Henry: 'the king's son' flatters Amnon's rank and the abuse-of-power maxim that will is law.
Gill: Amnon owns his lust and miscalls it love, naming Tamar as Absalom's sister.
Gill: the subtle Jonadab at once devises a scheme to open a way to Amnon's desire.
Keil-Delitzsch and Ellicott: the counsel is craftily framed, and 'feign thyself sick' exploits the real pining already described.
Wesley: the words 'my sister' and 'at her hand' are chosen to disarm suspicion and isolate Tamar.
Gill: Amnon acts on Jonadab's advice, feigning sickness.
Ellicott: the request seemed natural and was granted; 'sent home' means into the women's apartments.
Henry: David's fatherly love, uncoupled from watchfulness, walks into the snare, God hiding his heart from understanding.
Gill: Tamar was likely at Absalom's house, and David sends for her with an innocent command.
Keil-Delitzsch narrate the plain sequence, letting David's unwitting role carry the dramatic irony.
Gill: Tamar comes in obedience and affection, with an innocent breast and no suspicion.
Henry: a king's daughter's homely industry commends good housewifery and charity to the sick (Prov. 31:13).
Gill: Tamar poured out the cakes in his sight as a careful nurse; he refused and cleared the room.
Keil-Delitzsch and Ellicott: each sinister step wears the face of a whimsical invalid's fancy, so Tamar suspects nothing.
Henry: the adulterer is in care that no eye see him (Job 24:15); the sick man's licence clears the last witness.

themes: the licence of the sick abused

schools.summary: Gill: the inner chamber is chosen for secrecy, out of reach of the household.
Keil-Delitzsch bring the narrative to the seizure: 'Come, lie with me, my sister.'
Wesley reconstructs the removes from couch to outer room to secret chamber, drawing Tamar from safety to danger.
Gill: Tamar was drawn to the very couch-side, within reach when Amnon cast off disguise.
Henry: the mask thrown off, Amnon insults her known virtue, for the unclean think others like themselves.
Gill: 'Nay, my brother' is itself an argument from the forbidden nearness of the relation.
Clarke calls the speech tender and persuasive but lost on a brute; Wesley unpacks 'brother,' 'force,' and 'in Israel.'
Keil-Delitzsch and Ellicott trace her plea to Gen. 34:7 and Israel's law of holiness (Lev. 20:8).
Gill: Tamar weighs her ruined reputation and Amnon's coming disgrace as 'one of the fools in Israel.'
Clarke and Wesley: she may have genuinely thought David would dispense with the law to save his firstborn, or spoke to gain escape.
Ellicott: the marriage was forbidden (Lev. 18:9; 20:17), so she spoke only to gain time; Keil-Delitzsch mark that force followed.

debates.question: Did Tamar really expect the king to grant her in marriage, or was she only playing for time?

sides.position: she genuinely thought David might dispense with the law to save his firstborn son
the marriage was clearly forbidden; she made any suggestion merely to gain time and escape

schools.summary: Gill: Amnon's inflamed lust could attend to no argument, and he would not hearken.
Keil-Delitzsch record the deed with restraint: being stronger, he forced her.
Henry: such wickedness argues a long unchecked course of lewdness; strength alone prevailed over virtue's every reason.

themes: the deed veiled in few words

schools.summary: Keil-Delitzsch: the change proves lust is not love but gratification of animal passion; Ellicott adds that men hate those they injure.
Gill: a stung conscience and Tamar's reproaches turned Amnon's lust into loathing of its object.
Clarke calls it brutal and inexplicable; Wesley reads the hatred as God's just judgment to make the sin public.
Gill gives the plain sense: there was no cause for such treatment.
Keil-Delitzsch and Ellicott: expulsion is the greater wrong, making Tamar appear guilty and breaking Amnon's duty to shelter her (Deut. 22:27).
Wesley: though the rape was the greater sin, the expulsion was the greater cruelty, turning a private offence into public scandal.

themes: the second wrong worse than the first

schools.summary: Gill: Amnon summons the servant who ministered to him to remove Tamar.
Keil-Delitzsch and Ellicott: 'put out this person' and the bolted door stage a slander, making Tamar appear the guilty one.
Keil-Delitzsch and Ellicott: the ketoneth passim (as in Gen. 37:3) marked her a royal virgin, yet she was treated as a common woman.
Clarke refers to Joseph's coat; Wesley glosses the robe as embroidered work.
Gill canvasses the robe's make and notes that such garments elsewhere marked harlots, not virgins.
Keil-Delitzsch and Ellicott read each gesture as a sign of grief and a proper public means to seek justice.
Wesley: the ashes, covered head, and loud crying manifest her abhorrence and that it was not done by her consent.
Gill: the ashes are the customary token of sorrow and distress.

themes: the hand of God upon her

schools.summary: Keil-Delitzsch and Ellicott: Absalom's euphemistic question shows he divined the truth; he comforts her but counsels silence to serve his planned revenge.
Gill supplies the setting, Absalom speaking when Tamar reached his house.
Wesley notes the modesty of the phrase and unmasks Absalom's reasons for silence as veils over his design of vengeance.
Keil-Delitzsch and Ellicott: David failed the law's demand (Lev. 20:17) from indulgent love, the LXX's added clause, and his own guilty conscience.
Wesley: David did not punish Amnon as he should, from consciousness of his own guilt or foolish indulgence.
Henry: David fell into Eli's error (1 Sam. 3:13), letting anger serve instead of justice, which hardens sinners (Eccl. 8:11).
Gill: Absalom talked freely and friendly on other things to conceal his hatred and his design of revenge.
Wesley: total silence would have raised jealousy, so Absalom feigned brotherly kindness while nursing vengeance.
Henry: hidden, gilded, long-harboured malice is the worst; to hate the person is to be a murderer already (1 John 3:15).
Gill: the two-year silence was designed so the family would think the wrong forgotten and confide in Absalom.
Wesley: the delay both aggravates his implacable malice and serves as policy to lull Amnon and David.
Keil-Delitzsch, Clarke, and Ellicott set the sheep-shearing feast (1 Sam. 25:2) at Baal-hazor and note the delay disarmed suspicion and gave David time to act.
Gill: Absalom comes in person to invite David, posing as the dutiful son.
Keil-Delitzsch and Ellicott: David declines but blesses him; pressing the king disguised Absalom's real purpose and secured his goodwill.
Henry: the invitation mixes ambition and display with the darker gathering purpose.
Gill: David is willing that some go but not all, likely excepting himself and his heir Amnon.
Henry: David declines to spare Absalom expense, an example to parents to give children a competency and keep them from living above it.

themes: kindness turned to the plot's use

schools.summary: Gill: 'if not' is Absalom's fallback, asking that Amnon go if the king will not.
Keil-Delitzsch leave open whether David suspected; Ellicott thinks he consented rather than voice an unproven suspicion.
Clarke and Wesley: Amnon's rank as heir was made the plausible pretext; David meant to keep him home.

debates.question: Did David feel a suspicion when Absalom singled out Amnon?

sides.position: the long delay had made David feel wholly secure; suspicion cannot be affirmed
David hesitated from a suspicion he was unwilling to express, and consented rather than voice it

schools.summary: Gill: David's mind was blinded that Amnon's incest be punished and Nathan's threatening (2 Sam. 12:10) fulfilled.
Wesley: God suffered the wise king to be blinded, to judge David and bring Amnon the just punishment of his lewdness.
Keil-Delitzsch and Ellicott record the yielding and the LXX gloss, 'Absalom made a feast like a king's.'
Gill: Absalom pre-arranged the signal, ordering the servants to strike Amnon at his word and fear not.
Wesley: Amnon is struck when merry and off guard, the heir promising his servants pardon and reward.
Henry catalogues the aggravations: murder when merry with wine, servants made complicit, God's command defied, and an eye toward the throne.
Gill: the servants smote and killed Amnon at Absalom's word.
Keil-Delitzsch and Ellicott: the king's sons fled on mules; the servants obeyed unquestioning, Absalom now next in line if Chileab was dead.
Wesley: the threatened sword (2 Sam. 12:10) is now drawn in David's house, the father accessory by his connivance.
Keil-Delitzsch note the exaggerated report; Ellicott reads its lifelike accuracy as a mark of contemporaneous authorship.
Henry: fame makes bad worse; be not crushed by unconfirmed evil tidings, yet David's grief was real.
Clarke enlarges on rumour with Virgil's Fama, the plague that gains strength by going.
Gill likens David's torn garments to Jacob's grief over Joseph's coat (Gen. 37:34), believing all his sons slain.
Keil-Delitzsch picture king and courtiers rent and standing motionless; Ellicott notes 'rent his clothes' is the same act for both.

themes: torn robes of father and daughter

schools.summary: Gill identifies Jonadab as the same man who contrived the rape, now reassuring the king.
Keil-Delitzsch and Ellicott: Jonadab divined Absalom's fixed purpose, 'set upon his mouth' from the day of the rape.
Clarke marvels that so bad a man speaks coolly of the tragedy he had helped contrive.
Henry: Jonadab's foreknowledge indicts him; failing to prevent mischief makes one accessory (Prov. 24:12).
Gill: Jonadab urges the king not to take it to heart, for Amnon only is dead.
Keil-Delitzsch place vv. 30-33 as a parenthesis describing the court's impression before the sons arrive.
Gill: Absalom could not protect himself, knowing wilful murder was death by law with no city of refuge.
Keil-Delitzsch and Ellicott place the flight after v. 29 and explain the watchman's sighting 'behind him,' from the west.
Henry: Absalom now fears the king's sons as they feared him; no refuge shelters a wilful murderer.
Gill: as they neared, the company was seen to be the king's sons, vindicating Jonadab's word.
Keil-Delitzsch: the sons arrive weeping the moment Jonadab speaks, and the whole court weeps sore.
Gill marks the timing: the sons came weeping the instant Jonadab finished speaking.
Keil-Delitzsch: the coincidence of word and event shows the narrative's order, the court's pent grief breaking out.
Gill: the flight is repeated for the sake of what follows.
Keil-Delitzsch and Ellicott: Talmai was Absalom's maternal grandfather (2 Sam. 3:3), and the son mourned daily is Amnon.
Clarke and Wesley: as a wilful murderer Absalom must leave Israel, fleeing to Talmai for protection and mediation toward pardon.

themes: guilty son safe, grieving father at home

schools.summary: Gill relays Abarbinel: fearing David's vengeance, Absalom ceased going abroad and stayed in fortified Geshur three years.
Ellicott and Keil-Delitzsch: each repetition of the flight adds a fact, here that he remained three years, time softening David's grief.
Gill keeps the received sense: David, comforted concerning Amnon, longed after his surviving son Absalom.
Clarke and Wesley read paternal longing and lament it as the weakness of Eli, honouring sons above justice.
Ellicott and Keil-Delitzsch take the verb impersonally: David desisted from going forth against Absalom, his grief for Amnon fading.

debates.question: Does the verse mean David longed for Absalom, or that he desisted from going out against him?

sides.position: David's soul longed after Absalom in paternal affection (the received English, Chaldee, and Jewish reading)
David ceased from going forth against Absalom to punish him; 'the soul of' is not in the text
